title: WishGxd: Toronto's quietest cinematic voice
author: Allastair Voss
body:
Juqan Brown is twenty-two and from Scarborough, which is the part of Toronto most rap fans skip when they list the city's exports. He records as WishGxd. The sound is moody, melodic, and shot through with a strange patience for a young artist working in trap. He is in no rush to tell you what the song is about. He would rather build the room first.

[spotify:artist/3yj7Msyik9dDL4u6EC1mAP]

The 2024 single "BADLANDS" was the first time most listeners noticed. It opens with a synth pad that sounds like the inside of a hotel room at four in the morning and then refuses to escalate the way trap usually does. The 2025 debut EP IN LIVING COLOR built on that approach. "Limelight" became the most-streamed cut and it earns it: the hook is barely sung, the production is doing the heavy emotional lifting, and the writing trusts you to lean in.

## The Sound in Detail

Brown's production approach centers on texture before rhythm. Most trap music is built from the drums outward, with the hi-hat patterns and kick placements establishing the energy before melodic elements enter. WishGxd inverts this. The synth environment comes first, establishing an emotional temperature, and the percussion arrives later as confirmation rather than foundation.

The 808s in his tracks are pitched higher than the genre standard, sitting in a register that is less about physical bass impact and more about harmonic color. They function as melodic elements as much as rhythmic ones, moving through chord tones in a way that thickens the arrangement without adding explicit instrumentation. This is a sophisticated use of a production tool that most producers treat as a pure rhythm instrument.

The vocal approach reinforces this. Brown rarely raps in a conventional sense, using a melodic delivery that sits between singing and spoken word, closer to the PARTYNEXTDOOR lineage than to drill or straightforward trap. The pitch choices are unusual. He does not resolve to the obvious note. He finds the adjacent chord tone, the one that implies tension rather than release, and stays there.

## Scarborough as Sonic Context

The geography matters. Scarborough sits east of Toronto's downtown core and carries a demographic reality, majority-immigrant, majority working-class, that the city's music industry has historically underrepresented even while benefiting from the talent it produces. The Weeknd's origin story runs through Scarborough. So does a generation of producers and songwriters whose names appear in credits without appearing in promotional photographs.

Brown is not making political music. He is not making music about Scarborough specifically. But the patience in his work, the refusal to optimize for immediate gratification, the willingness to build a mood before making a point, reflects something about an environment where you are not the default subject of mainstream attention. You develop your own standards because the mainstream has no category ready for you.

## The Frank Ocean and PARTYNEXTDOOR Thread

"ambition weighs heavy" is the most exposed thing he has put out. It is a young man's song about wanting things and the cost of that wanting, sung over a beat that will not let you forget it. There is a thread that connects this track to early Frank Ocean, to PARTYNEXTDOOR's first mixtape, to that small group of artists who realized R&B and trap could share a room without arguing.

The comparison to Frank Ocean is not made carelessly. Both artists approach the vocal as a textural element as much as a narrative one. Both use production space, the gaps and absences in the arrangement, as expressive tools. Both arrived with a fully formed aesthetic at an age when most artists are still assembling one. Brown is earlier in his career than Ocean was on Nostalgia/Ultra, but the instinct is recognizably related.

## The IN LIVING COLOR EP

The five-track EP moves from "BADLANDS" through "Limelight" with enough variation to demonstrate range and enough consistency to establish an identity. The closing track is the most ambitious, a four-minute piece that shifts tempo and texture twice without losing the thread. Brown is not yet making albums that test structural patience over forty minutes. He will be.

The EP has been picked up by taste-making playlists and referenced in coverage of Toronto's evolving sound. His streaming numbers are modest by industry standards. They are exactly right for an artist at this stage: enough to confirm that the work is connecting with the right people, not so large that they create the expectation problem that many artists struggle with after early viral success.

## What Cinematic Actually Means Here

The word cinematic gets overused to describe moody R&B, but for WishGxd it is accurate in a specific technical sense. His tracks have opening sequences, development sections, and conclusions. They are structured like short films, with an establishing shot, a middle that builds information, and an ending that recontextualizes what came before. "BADLANDS" does this in under three minutes. That kind of compression is compositional skill, not lucky production.

The synth pads that open most of his tracks function as establishing shots in this sense. They set a location and a temperature before anything else happens. By the time the rhythm enters, you are already inside the world the song is building. The transition from introduction to body never feels like a drop or a reveal. It feels like a natural deepening of something that was already present. That smoothness is harder to execute than it sounds. It requires knowing precisely where to place every element and having the discipline not to front-load the arrangement with information that should arrive later. Brown has that discipline at twenty-two. It is the most striking thing about the work.

## Why Now

Toronto rap has been famous for one mood for over a decade. WishGxd is part of a small generation pulling at the edges of that mood and finding more there than the city's biggest exports admitted to. "Limelight," "BADLANDS," and "ambition weighs heavy" form a small early canon. Listen in that order. The sound is patient. So is he.
